title: Evaluation, Postcleaning, and Documentation Workshop
narrationScript: You have found an indication, proved it repeats, and called it relevant. Now the part needs a verdict, and the verdict is not yours to invent. Evaluation means measuring what you found and comparing it to acceptance criteria that come from the governing code, the customer specification, and your procedure. This lesson walks the closeout of an inspection. The decision, the cleanup, and the paperwork. Start with classification. Most surface criteria sort relevant indications into linear and rounded. A common definition calls an indication linear when its length is at least three times its width. Everything else is rounded. Then measure. Length, location, orientation, and spacing from its neighbors. A typical pressure equipment style criteria set reads like this. No cracks, period. Relevant linear indications longer than one sixteenth of an inch are rejected. Relevant rounded indications larger than three sixteenths of an inch are rejected. Four or more rounded indications in a line, separated by one sixteenth of an inch or less, are rejected. Aerospace work is usually tighter, often rejecting any crack like indication outright. The exact numbers change with every code and every part class. The discipline does not. Measure, compare, document. Acceptance and rejection both have mechanics. An accepted part is documented and released, and where the procedure requires it, even accepted indications get recorded. A rejected part gets the indication marked, using a marker your procedure allows, because some alloys cannot tolerate certain marking materials on their surfaces. The part is tagged, segregated so it cannot drift back into good stock, and documented. Rejection is rarely the end of the story. Most parts come back after grinding or weld repair, and the rule is simple. The repaired area is re examined by the same method, to the same procedure and criteria, before release. The loop closes on a passing retest, not on a promise. When you are unsure, doubt goes up the ladder to a Level Two or a Level Three, never into the accept bin. Now the cleanup. Particles and their carrier are contamination to the next operation. Powder in an oil passage, fluorescent bath under paint, iron dust on a bearing fit. All of them cause trouble. The typical closeout order runs record, demagnetize, clean, protect. Demagnetize first when it is required, because a residual field holds particles and fights your cleaning. Verify with a field indicator, commonly to three gauss or less, or whatever your procedure states, and remember for scale that the earth's field sits near half a gauss. Then remove the particles. Dry powder blows or brushes off. Wet bath rinses away with the right solvent or detergent. Protection comes last and it comes quickly. Parts tested in a water based bath can flash rust within hours, so corrosion preventive goes on promptly. Replace any coatings you stripped, cap and plug finished bores, and handle machined surfaces like they cost money, because they do. Finally, the record. A magnetic particle report exists so that a stranger can repeat your test exactly and reach the same verdict. That means real values, not adjectives. Part and job identification. Procedure number and revision. Equipment and serial numbers with calibration status. Technique, meaning method, current type, amperage for every shot, field directions, and coverage. Particle type and batch, bath concentration from the settling test, and the light meter readings. Field verification, like the shim or the gauge you used. Results, with a sketch or map showing each indication's location, length, and orientation. The disposition. And your name, your certification level, your signature, and the date. If it is not written down, it did not happen. Write it down while the part is still in front of you, close the package, and the inspection is done. That habit, more than any single reading, is what makes your work stand up months later when someone pulls the file and asks what you saw and how you knew.
imagePrompt: A technician slides a finished gear shaft out of a tabletop demagnetizing coil beside a workbench where cleaned, lightly oiled steel parts are racked for release in an industrial inspection shop.

sections.heading: Evaluation: From Indication to Disposition
sections.body:
Interpretation told you the indication is **relevant** — caused by a real discontinuity. **Evaluation** decides what that means for the part, and it is a measuring job, not a judgment call. First classify: most surface criteria divide relevant indications into **linear** (length at least **3× the width**) and **rounded** (everything else). Then measure length, note location and orientation, and check spacing to neighboring indications.

The yardstick comes from the governing code or specification, flowed down through your procedure — never from personal experience. A typical pressure-equipment-style criteria set reads: **no cracks of any size**; relevant **linear** indications longer than **1/16 in.** rejected; relevant **rounded** indications larger than **3/16 in.** rejected; **four or more rounded** indications in a line separated by **1/16 in. or less** rejected. Aerospace criteria are usually tighter — often any crack-like indication is rejectable outright, with engineering review for anything ambiguous. The numbers move from code to code and class to class; the discipline never does: **measure, compare, document**. If an indication sits at the limit, it is measured again, witnessed, or bumped up — not rounded down.

Measure with a real tool — a graduated scale or measuring magnifier held at the indication, under the same lighting that revealed it. And measure honestly: particle buildup **exaggerates width**, so classification uses the indication's proportions as found, while length is read end-to-end of the particle line. When criteria say "separated by," measure edge-to-edge between indications. Sloppy measurement turns a borderline reject into a shipped defect.

sections.caption: The disposition path every indication follows: false patterns exit to technique fixes, nonrelevant to verification, and relevant indications to measurement against the criteria.

sections.heading: Making the Call — and What Happens After
sections.body:
**Acceptance** is not just a stamp: the part is documented and released, and where the procedure requires, even accepted relevant indications are recorded with size and location so the next inspection has a baseline.

**Rejection** has mechanics. Mark the indication location with a marker your procedure allows — many shops restrict marking materials on stainless, nickel, and titanium alloys, so use the approved pen, not whatever is in your pocket. Tag the part, segregate it so it cannot drift back into good stock, and document what, where, and how big.

Rejection is rarely final. Most rejected parts return after **rework or repair** — grinding out a crack, weld repair under its own approved procedure — and the rule is absolute: the repaired area is **re-examined by the same method, to the same procedure and criteria**, before release. The loop closes on a passing re-test, not a promise. Two boundaries on your authority: doubt goes **up the ladder** to a Level II or III, never into the accept bin; and decisions to use a nonconforming part as-is are **engineering dispositions** that arrive in writing — a technician neither waives nor tightens criteria.

sections.caption: A pressure-equipment-style surface criteria set — illustration only; the governing code edition and part class always rule.
rows: 4 or more in a line, spaced ≤ 1/16 in. apart, not permitted
Geometry or structure confirmed as the source

sections.heading: Postcleaning and Protection
sections.body:
To the next operation, your particles are **contamination**. Dry powder in an oil gallery, fluorescent bath trapped under new paint, iron dust on a bearing journal — each one is a failure you caused after the inspection passed. So postcleaning is part of the test, and the typical closeout order is **record → demagnetize → clean → protect**.

Demagnetize first where required, because a residual field grips the very particles you are trying to remove (and later attracts chips during machining, deflects arcs during welding, and disturbs instruments). Verify with a field indicator — commonly **3 gauss or less**, or per procedure; the earth's field of roughly **0.5 gauss** gives you a sense of scale. Then remove the particles: dry powder blows off with low-pressure air or brushes away; wet bath drains and rinses with the appropriate solvent or detergent wash.

**Protection** follows immediately. Parts examined in water-based vehicles can **flash-rust within hours**, so corrosion preventive goes on promptly. Oil-vehicle residue may need degreasing before painting or plating. Replace any protective coating you stripped for the test, cap or plug finished bores and threads, and handle ground surfaces like the money they represent.

sections.title: If it is not recorded, it did not happen
sections.body: Reports carry **numbers, not adjectives**: actual amperage per shot, actual UV-A and white-light readings, actual bath concentration — never just "OK." The record is retained per the contract and the governing practice, and it may be the only witness years later when a part, a weld, or a decision is questioned.

sections.heading: Documentation of the Test
sections.body:
A complete MT record lets a stranger repeat your examination exactly and reach the same verdict. The typical minimum:

- **Identification** — part number, serial or heat, job and purchase order, date, and the governing code or spec.
- **Procedure** — procedure number and revision, plus the technique sheet used.
- **Equipment** — bench, yoke, or power pack with serial numbers and calibration status; ammeter, radiometer, light meter, and field indicator in calibration.
- **Technique** — method (continuous or residual), current type (AC, HWDC, FWDC), amperage or ampere-turns for **every shot**, field directions, and coverage.
- **Materials** — particle type and batch, vehicle, bath concentration from the settling test (typical ranges: visible **1.2–2.4 mL per 100 mL**, fluorescent **0.1–0.4 mL per 100 mL**), and light readings.
- **Verification** — QQI shim or gaussmeter results proving field adequacy.
- **Results** — a sketch, map, or photo locating each relevant indication with length and orientation; nonrelevant findings noted with cause.
- **Disposition and credentials** — accept/reject, inspector name, certification level, signature, date.

Write it while the part is in front of you — memory is not a record.

sections.caption: Minimum contents of an MT inspection record — built so anyone can repeat the test and reach the same answer.

interactive.title: Accept, Reject, or Rework
interactive.intro: You are closing out wet fluorescent examinations on overhauled pump shafts and welded brackets. The governing criteria for this job: no cracks of any size; relevant linear indications over 1/16 in. rejected; relevant rounded indications over 3/16 in. rejected. Make each call.

steps.prompt: A sharp, dense 3/16-in. linear indication sits across the bearing-journal fillet of a shaft. It returns after you wipe the area and re-apply bath with current on. Your call?
options: Nonrelevant — fillets always hold a few particles
Relevant linear, probable crack — reject, mark with the approved marker, and document size and location
False — drainage from the fillet radius; release the shaft
steps.feedback: Reproducible, sharp, and dense at a stress riser is a probable fatigue crack. At 3/16 in. it exceeds the 1/16-in. linear limit — and a crack is rejectable at any size under these criteria. It gets marked, tagged, segregated, and documented.

steps.prompt: On a welded bracket you find a single rounded indication about 1/8 in. across, well clear of any others, confirmed relevant.
options: Reject — every relevant indication is a defect
Accept — 1/8 in. is under the 3/16-in. rounded limit; record it per the procedure and release
Grind it out immediately without recording anything
steps.feedback: Evaluation means comparing to the criteria, not to your mood: 1/8 in. is below the 3/16-in. rounded limit, so the bracket is acceptable. A relevant indication that is accepted still gets recorded where the procedure requires it.

steps.prompt: A shaft you rejected last week comes back from weld repair with a fresh, ground-flush buildup on the journal. The next step is:
options: Visual check only — it was already examined by MT once
Re-examine the repaired area by MT to the same procedure and criteria before release
Release it — the repair shop performs its own checks
steps.feedback: Repaired areas are re-examined with the same method, procedure, and acceptance criteria. The reject-repair loop closes on a passing re-test, never on the repair ticket.

steps.prompt: A batch of accepted shafts comes off the water-vehicle bath line late Friday, headed for storage until Monday. Closeout?
options: Box them as they sit — they passed
Record results, demagnetize and verify with a field indicator (commonly 3 G or less, or per procedure), postclean the bath residue, then apply rust preventive
Skip demagnetization — the residual field is invisible, so it cannot matter
steps.feedback: Water-based vehicles flash-rust steel within hours, and a residual field holds particles and attracts chips in later machining. The closeout order is record, demag with verification, clean, protect — then the paperwork is finished while the parts are still in front of you.

checkQuestions.question: Under common surface-examination criteria, an indication is classified as linear when:
options: Its length is at least three times its width
It is longer than 1 inch regardless of width
It fluoresces brighter than its background
Its width is at least three times its length
checkQuestions.explanation: The widely used convention defines a linear indication as one whose length is at least 3× its width; everything else is treated as rounded. Classification comes before any size comparison.

checkQuestions.question: Criteria reject relevant linear indications longer than 1/16 in. You measure a relevant linear indication at 3/32 in. The disposition is:
options: Accept — it is under the limit
Reject — 3/32 in. (6/64) exceeds 1/16 in. (4/64)
Accept if no rounded indications are nearby
Re-classify it as rounded and accept
checkQuestions.explanation: Convert to a common denominator: 3/32 = 6/64 and 1/16 = 4/64, so the indication exceeds the limit and the part is rejected and documented. Evaluation is arithmetic against the criteria, not impression.

checkQuestions.question: The typical order of closeout operations after interpretation is complete is:
options: Clean, demagnetize, record, protect
Record results, demagnetize and verify (commonly 3 G or less, or per procedure), postclean, then protect
Protect first so corrosion never starts, then clean
Demagnetization is unnecessary if the parts look clean
checkQuestions.explanation: Results are recorded before anything disturbs them; demag comes before cleaning because a residual field holds particles; verification uses a field indicator; protection follows cleaning immediately.

checkQuestions.question: Why is postcleaning followed immediately by protection on parts examined with a water-based bath?
options: Purely for appearance at final inspection
Residual particles and vehicle interfere with painting, plating, machining, and assembly — and water vehicles can flash-rust steel within hours
Only fluorescent baths ever need removal
Postcleaning re-magnetizes the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Particles and carrier contaminate later operations (paint, plating, bearings, oil passages), and water-based vehicles leave steel ready to flash-rust, so corrosion preventive is applied promptly after cleaning.

checkQuestions.question: Which of these is a complete enough MT record?
options: "MT performed — acceptable," with a date

Part ID, procedure and revision, equipment with calibration status, technique with amperage and field directions per shot, particle batch and bath concentration, light readings, verification, results map, disposition, and inspector name, level, signature, and date
The amperage used, recorded on the work order
A photograph of the part replaces all written data
checkQuestions.explanation: The record must let someone else repeat the examination exactly and reach the same verdict — identification, technique values, materials, lighting, verification, mapped results, disposition, and credentials.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §12.8 (evaluation, criteria, acceptance/rejection)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §12.9 (postcleaning and protection)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §12.10 (documentation of test)
Acceptance-criteria style of pressure-equipment codes such as ASME BPVC Section VIII (generic reference)
Records and postcleaning practice in standards such as ASTM E1444 (generic reference)
